questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Constructive eviction - tenant vacates after landlord fails to remedy serious interference
questions.stem: A business leased office space for five years. A month into the lease, a defect in the building's electrical system caused the office to lose power repeatedly, making ordinary business operations impossible. The tenant promptly notified the landlord, who had the duty to maintain the building systems but refused to make repairs. After three weeks of severe outages, the tenant vacated the space and stopped paying rent. The landlord sued for future rent. The jurisdiction applies common-law constructive eviction principles.
questions.call: Is the tenant likely relieved of future rent liability?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the landlord's failure to remedy a substantial interference caused the tenant to vacate within a reasonable time.
choices.rationale: Constructive eviction requires substantial interference attributable to the landlord and vacation of the premises within a reasonable time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because any minor inconvenience in leased premises automatically ends the lease.
choices.rationale: Constructive eviction requires a substantial interference, not a minor inconvenience.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because constructive eviction is available only when the landlord physically locks out the tenant.
choices.rationale: Physical exclusion is actual eviction; constructive eviction can result from serious interference with use and enjoyment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the tenant had to remain in possession and sue for damages while continuing to pay full rent.
choices.rationale: Under constructive eviction doctrine, the tenant generally must vacate to terminate the rent obligation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a commercial tenant may claim constructive eviction after severe unrepaired power outages.
explanation.governingRule: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord's wrongful act or failure to act substantially interferes with the tenant's use and enjoyment, and the tenant vacates within a reasonable time.
explanation.application: Repeated power loss made the office unusable, the landlord refused repairs despite duty and notice, and the tenant left after three weeks.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing constructive eviction with actual lockout or with minor repair disputes.
explanation.memoryAid: Serious landlord-caused interference plus timely move-out can end rent.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Recording acts - wild deed outside chain of title
questions.stem: Owner conveyed land to Buyer 1, who did not record. Buyer 1 later conveyed the land to Buyer 2, who immediately recorded that deed. Because Buyer 1's deed from Owner had not been recorded, Buyer 2's deed did not appear in the chain of title from Owner. Owner then conveyed the same land to Buyer 3 for value. At that time, Buyer 3 had no actual knowledge of either earlier conveyance, no one was in possession, and Buyer 3 promptly recorded. The jurisdiction has a notice recording statute.
questions.call: Who has superior title?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Buyer 2, because Buyer 2 recorded before Buyer 3 acquired the land.
choices.rationale: A deed recorded outside the chain of title generally does not give constructive notice to a later purchaser searching from the common grantor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Buyer 3, because Buyer 3 was a subsequent purchaser for value without notice under a notice statute.
choices.rationale: Buyer 2's recorded deed was wild because Buyer 1's deed was unrecorded, so Buyer 3 lacked record notice and qualifies as a bona fide purchaser.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Buyer 1, because the first conveyance from Owner is always superior regardless of recording acts.
choices.rationale: Recording statutes protect qualifying later purchasers against prior unrecorded interests.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Buyer 2, but only because Buyer 3 recorded after taking title.
choices.rationale: Under a notice statute, the key is Buyer 3's lack of notice when taking; Buyer 2's wild deed does not provide notice.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a recorded deed outside the chain of title gives constructive notice.
explanation.governingRule: A wild deed recorded outside the chain of title generally does not provide constructive notice. Under a notice statute, a subsequent purchaser for value without notice prevails over prior unrecorded interests.
explanation.application: Buyer 2 recorded before Buyer 1's deed from Owner, so the record did not connect Buyer 2 to Owner's chain. Buyer 3 bought for value without actual, inquiry, or record notice.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming every recorded document gives notice to the world no matter where it appears.
explanation.memoryAid: Record first matters only if the record is in the chain.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Express easement scope - use beyond residential access
questions.stem: A deed granted the owner of Lot A an express easement over a driveway on Lot B 'for residential ingress and egress to Lot A.' Lot A was then a single-family home. Years later, the owner of Lot A converted the property into a commercial event venue and began sending shuttle buses and delivery trucks over the driveway several times each day. The increased use did not physically damage the driveway but substantially changed the character of the traffic. The owner of Lot B sued to limit the use.
questions.call: Is the owner of Lot B likely to obtain relief?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because any express easement permits every use that is physically possible on the servient land.
choices.rationale: The scope of an express easement is determined by the grant's terms and reasonable expectations, not physical possibility alone.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the servient owner must prove actual physical damage before objecting to an expanded use.
choices.rationale: A use may exceed the easement's scope even without physical damage if it is outside the granted purpose or materially changes the burden.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because using the driveway for a commercial event venue exceeds the easement granted for residential ingress and egress.
choices.rationale: The grant expressly limits the easement to residential access, and commercial bus and delivery use materially exceeds that scope.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only by terminating the entire easement rather than limiting the excessive use.
choices.rationale: The usual remedy for overuse is to enjoin or limit the excessive use, not necessarily extinguish the easement altogether.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an express residential access easement may be expanded to serve commercial venue traffic.
explanation.governingRule: The scope of an express easement is governed by the instrument's terms, construed in light of the circumstances. The dominant owner may not materially exceed the granted purpose or burden the servient estate beyond the easement's scope.
explanation.application: The deed granted residential ingress and egress for Lot A. Converting the use to commercial event traffic with buses and delivery trucks exceeds the stated residential purpose.
explanation.commonTrap: Looking only for physical damage and ignoring the words of the easement.
explanation.memoryAid: Express easements are bounded by their grant.
